question: A wave of new technology raises the marginal productivity of capital, increasing firms' demand for loanable funds at every interest rate, while the saving curve is unchanged. In the diagram shown (I shifts to I', S fixed), what happens to the equilibrium real interest rate and the level of saving/investment?
choices: The real interest rate rises, and the equilibrium level of saving and investment both rise
The real interest rate falls, and the equilibrium level of saving and investment both fall
The real interest rate rises, but the equilibrium level of saving and investment both fall
The real interest rate is unaffected, since saving didn't shift
correctExplanation: Higher marginal productivity of capital increases investment demand at every interest rate, shifting the investment demand curve right (I to I'). With saving unchanged, the new equilibrium E' is both a higher interest rate and a higher quantity of saving and investment -- the higher rate induces more saving (a movement along the fixed saving curve) to fund the higher investment demand, exactly as Class 5 describes.
wrongExplanations: This has both directions backwards -- a rightward shift in investment demand raises the interest rate and raises saving/investment, not lowers them.
The interest rate does rise, but saving and investment both rise as a result (not fall) -- the higher rate draws out more saving to match the higher investment demand.
The interest rate does change even though saving itself doesn't shift, because the equilibrium point moves along the (unshifted) saving curve in response to the shift in investment demand.

diagram.alt: A loanable funds diagram with saving and investment on the horizontal axis and the real interest rate on the vertical axis. The investment demand curve I shifts right to I', while the saving curve S stays fixed. Equilibrium moves from E to E', with both the real interest rate and the level of saving and investment rising.
diagram.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 270 190" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="presentation" focusable="false"><line x1="50" y1="20" x2="50" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="230" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140.0" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Saving and Investment</text><text x="12" y="90.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)" transform="rotate(-90 12 90.0)">Real Interest Rate</text><line x1="60" y1="30" x2="210" y2="150" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><line x1="90" y1="30" x2="240" y2="150" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/><line x1="60" y1="150" x2="210" y2="30" stroke="var(--good)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><circle cx="135" cy="90" r="3.2" fill="var(--text)"/><circle cx="150" cy="78" r="3.2" fill="var(--text)"/><text x="122" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">E</text><text x="163" y="65" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">E'</text><text x="213" y="33" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">S</text><text x="213" y="153" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">I</text><text x="243" y="153" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">I'</text></svg>

question: A student looks at the loanable funds diagram for the budget-deficit scenario (S shifts left, I fixed) and says: 'The line labeled I must have shifted left too, since the equilibrium interest rate went up.' What is wrong with this interpretation?
choices: The interest rate rose because of a movement along the unchanged investment demand curve (caused by the saving shift), not because the investment demand curve itself shifted
The interest rate didn't actually rise in this scenario, so the student's premise is wrong
The I curve did shift, but it shifted right, not left
There's nothing wrong -- the I curve shifting left is a valid explanation for a higher interest rate
correctExplanation: A higher equilibrium interest rate can result from EITHER curve shifting -- a leftward shift of saving, or a leftward shift of investment demand, would each raise the equilibrium rate on its own. In this specific scenario, only the saving curve shifts (due to the larger deficit); the investment demand curve stays fixed, and the new equilibrium is found by moving along that same fixed curve. The student incorrectly assumes any interest-rate change must mean the OTHER curve shifted, rather than checking what actually caused the change.
wrongExplanations: The interest rate does rise in this scenario -- the crowding-out story explicitly involves a higher interest rate; the error is in what the student attributes the rise to, not whether it happened.
The investment demand curve doesn't shift at all in this scenario -- Class 5 attributes the crowding-out story entirely to a leftward shift in saving with investment demand held fixed.
This mistakes a movement along a fixed curve for a shift of that same curve -- a classic graph-reading error, not a valid alternative explanation.

question: In the loanable funds market, if the real interest rate is currently ABOVE its equilibrium level, what results?
choices: A surplus of saving (the quantity of saving supplied exceeds the quantity of investment funds demanded)
A shortage of saving (the quantity of investment funds demanded exceeds the quantity of saving supplied)
The market remains in equilibrium regardless of the interest rate
Both saving and investment demand curves shift until the surplus disappears
correctExplanation: Class 5 states that if the real interest rate is above equilibrium, there is a surplus of saving: at that high rate, savers want to supply more funds than borrowers want to demand at that rate, since saving increases and investment decreases as the rate rises above equilibrium.
wrongExplanations: A shortage results from a rate BELOW equilibrium, not above it -- above equilibrium produces a surplus, the opposite case.
The market is only in equilibrium exactly at the equilibrium interest rate; away from it (as here), there is a surplus or shortage.
A surplus is resolved by the interest rate itself falling back toward equilibrium (a movement along both curves), not by the curves shifting.

question: Two separate events both make investment more expensive for firms: (1) the real interest rate itself rises, and (2) the price of new capital equipment rises. In the loanable funds diagram, how do these two events differ in how they show up?
choices: A rise in the real interest rate is a movement along the (fixed) investment demand curve; a rise in the price of capital equipment shifts the investment demand curve left
Both events are movements along the investment demand curve; neither shifts it
Both events shift the investment demand curve left
A rise in the real interest rate shifts investment demand left; a rise in the price of capital equipment is a movement along the curve
correctExplanation: The real interest rate is the variable plotted on the loanable funds diagram's vertical axis, so a change in it moves the economy along the existing investment demand curve. The price of capital equipment is a determinant of the cost of investment that is NOT on either axis -- per Class 5, the cost of investment depends on both the price of capital goods and the real interest rate -- so a rise in the price of capital goods (holding the interest rate fixed) makes investment less attractive at every interest rate, shifting the investment demand curve left.
wrongExplanations: The price-of-capital-goods change is not captured by moving along the existing curve -- it changes how much investment firms want at every interest rate, which requires shifting the curve, not just moving along it.
Only the price-of-capital-goods change shifts the curve; the interest-rate change is a movement along the existing curve, not a shift.

This reverses the two -- the real interest rate is the axis variable (its own change means a movement along the curve), while the price of capital goods is an off-axis determinant (its change shifts the curve).

subtopic: Open Economy and S + KI = I
question: In a closed economy, the equilibrium real interest rate is determined by S = I alone. Now suppose the economy opens up and receives positive net capital inflows (KI > 0) at every interest rate, so the identity becomes S + KI = I. Compared to the closed-economy diagram, how does the open economy's loanable-funds diagram change, and what happens to the equilibrium real interest rate and investment?
choices: The effective supply of funds available for investment (S + KI) is greater than S alone at every interest rate, shifting the effective supply curve right; the equilibrium real interest rate is lower and investment is higher than in the closed economy
The effective supply of funds (S + KI) is smaller than S alone, shifting the effective supply curve left; the equilibrium real interest rate is higher and investment is lower
Capital inflows only affect the investment demand curve, shifting it right, while the supply curve is unaffected
Capital inflows have no graphical effect; they only appear in the NX + KI = 0 identity, not in the loanable funds diagram
correctExplanation: Since S + KI = I, positive net capital inflows add to domestic saving as a source of funds for investment at every interest rate, shifting the effective supply-of-funds curve (S + KI) to the right of the closed economy's S curve alone. Per Class 5's own statement that 'capital inflows mean more investment and lower interest rates,' the open economy reaches equilibrium at a lower real interest rate and a higher level of investment than the closed economy would with saving alone.
wrongExplanations: Positive net capital inflows ADD to the funds available for investment (foreign saving supplementing domestic saving), shifting effective supply right, not left -- this choice has the direction backwards.
Capital inflows are additional funds supplied for investment, not a change in firms' demand for those funds -- they affect the supply side (S + KI) of the diagram, not the investment demand curve.

Class 5 explicitly ties the S + KI = I identity to a graphical result (lower interest rate, more investment) -- capital inflows do have a direct effect on the loanable funds diagram, not just on the separate trade-balance identity.

question: Households become more worried about job security and sharply increase precautionary saving out of every dollar of income, while investment demand is unchanged. What happens to the equilibrium real interest rate and the level of investment?
choices: The real interest rate falls, and investment rises (a movement down along the fixed investment demand curve)
The real interest rate rises, and investment falls
The real interest rate falls, and investment also falls
Neither changes, since investment demand itself didn't shift
correctExplanation: An increase in precautionary saving shifts the saving curve right (more saving supplied at every interest rate). With investment demand unchanged, the new equilibrium sits lower on the same investment demand curve: the real interest rate falls, and the lower rate causes a movement along the unshifted investment demand curve to a higher quantity of investment -- the mirror image of the budget-deficit crowding-out story.
wrongExplanations: This has both directions backwards -- a rightward shift in saving lowers the interest rate and raises investment, not the reverse (that describes a leftward saving shift, like the budget-deficit case).
The interest rate does fall, but investment RISES as a result (not falls) -- the lower rate makes more investment projects profitable, moving the economy along the investment demand curve.

Even though investment demand itself doesn't shift, the equilibrium level of investment still changes, because the equilibrium point moves along the investment demand curve in response to the interest-rate change caused by the saving shift.

question: In the capital-inflow curve taught in Class 5, plotted against the domestic real interest rate, the curve slopes upward. What does this upward slope reflect?
choices: Higher domestic real interest rates make domestic assets more attractive to foreign investors, increasing capital inflows
Higher domestic real interest rates make domestic assets less attractive to foreign investors, decreasing capital inflows
Higher domestic real interest rates cause more domestic saving to flow abroad
Higher domestic real interest rates increase the trade deficit directly, with no role for foreign investors
correctExplanation: Class 5 states that higher domestic interest rates mean greater capital inflows, since foreign investors earn a better return on domestic assets relative to what's available elsewhere -- the key is the relative interest-rate difference. This is why the capital-inflow curve slopes upward in the domestic real interest rate.
wrongExplanations: This has the relationship backwards -- Class 5 states HIGHER domestic rates attract MORE capital inflows (make domestic assets more attractive), not fewer.
This describes capital OUTFLOWS (domestic savers buying foreign assets), which would actually decrease, not increase, as domestic rates become relatively more attractive.
The trade deficit is linked to capital inflows through the NX + KI = 0 identity, but the direct mechanism shown on this curve is investors' asset-purchase decisions, not the trade balance itself.

diagram.alt: A diagram with net capital inflows on the horizontal axis and the domestic real interest rate on the vertical axis. The capital-inflow curve KI slopes upward, showing that higher domestic interest rates attract more capital inflows.
diagram.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 190" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="presentation" focusable="false"><line x1="50" y1="20" x2="50" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="230" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140.0" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Net Capital Inflows (KI)</text><text x="12" y="90.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)" transform="rotate(-90 12 90.0)">Domestic Real Interest Rate</text><line x1="60" y1="150" x2="210" y2="30" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><text x="213" y="33" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">KI</text></svg>

question: Investors around the world become more worried about a country's political stability and the security of its property rights. In the diagram shown (KI shifts to KI'), what happens to that country's capital-inflow curve, holding the real interest rate fixed?
choices: The capital-inflow curve shifts left: at any given real interest rate, fewer capital inflows occur than before
The capital-inflow curve shifts right: at any given real interest rate, more capital inflows occur than before
The curve doesn't shift; there is only a movement along the existing curve
The capital-inflow curve becomes perfectly vertical, since risk eliminates any relationship with the interest rate
correctExplanation: Class 5 states directly that an increase in the perceived riskiness of domestic assets shifts the capital-inflow curve to the left: foreigners become less willing to buy the now-riskier domestic assets (and domestic savers become more willing to buy foreign assets instead), so capital inflows fall at every level of the real interest rate.
wrongExplanations: Class 5 states increased riskiness shifts the curve left (decreasing inflows at every rate), not right -- this choice has the direction backwards.

Risk is not the variable on either axis of this diagram (the axes are the real interest rate and net capital inflows), so a change in perceived risk shifts the whole curve rather than just moving along it.
Class 5 doesn't describe risk as eliminating the interest-rate relationship -- the curve keeps its normal upward slope, it just shifts to a less favorable position.

diagram.alt: A diagram with net capital inflows on the horizontal axis and the domestic real interest rate on the vertical axis. The capital-inflow curve shifts left from KI to KI', showing fewer capital inflows at every interest rate after a rise in perceived risk.
diagram.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 190" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="presentation" focusable="false"><line x1="50" y1="20" x2="50" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="230" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140.0" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Net Capital Inflows (KI)</text><text x="12" y="90.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)" transform="rotate(-90 12 90.0)">Domestic Real Interest Rate</text><line x1="60" y1="150" x2="210" y2="30" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><line x1="30" y1="150" x2="180" y2="30" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/><text x="213" y="33" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">KI</text><text x="26" y="148" text-anchor="end" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">KI'</text></svg>

question: A country's domestic real interest rate rises relative to rates available abroad, with no change in perceived risk. On the capital-inflow curve, does this represent a shift of the curve or a movement along it?
choices: A movement along the existing capital-inflow curve, since the real interest rate is the variable plotted on the vertical axis
A shift of the entire capital-inflow curve to the right
A shift of the entire capital-inflow curve to the left
Neither -- capital inflows are completely unresponsive to the domestic interest rate
correctExplanation: The capital-inflow curve is plotted with the domestic real interest rate on the vertical axis, so a change in that same interest rate -- holding risk and foreign returns fixed -- traces out a movement along the existing curve (to a higher point on the same upward-sloping curve), not a shift of the whole curve.
wrongExplanations: A curve shift requires a change in something NOT on either axis (like risk); a change in the interest rate itself, which IS the vertical axis variable, produces a movement along the curve instead.
Same issue as the other shift option -- an interest-rate change is a movement along the curve, not a shift, regardless of direction.
Class 5 explicitly grounds the capital-inflow curve's upward slope in interest-rate responsiveness -- capital inflows do respond to the domestic real interest rate.

question: A student sees that a country's domestic real interest rate rose and its capital inflows increased, and concludes: 'The capital-inflow curve must have shifted right, since capital inflows went up.' What is the error in this reasoning?
choices: If the interest rate itself rose and nothing else (like risk) changed, the increase in capital inflows is a movement along the unchanged curve, not evidence that the curve shifted
There is no error -- rising capital inflows always means the curve shifted right
The error is that capital inflows must have actually decreased, not increased
The error is that the curve shifted left, not right
correctExplanation: An increase in capital inflows can come from EITHER a movement along a fixed curve (caused by a change in the domestic interest rate itself) OR a rightward shift of the curve (caused by something else, like reduced risk). The student assumes any increase in capital inflows must mean the curve shifted, without checking what actually caused the change -- here, it was the interest rate itself rising, which is a movement along the existing curve.
wrongExplanations: This is exactly the error being tested -- rising capital inflows do NOT always mean the curve shifted; they can also result from a movement along a fixed curve when the interest rate itself changes.
The scenario states capital inflows increased; there's no reason to think they actually decreased.
The curve doesn't need to shift at all in this scenario -- the change in inflows is fully explained by the movement along the existing curve caused by the interest-rate change.

question: Compare two versions of the same economy's loanable funds diagram: one is a closed economy (equilibrium where S = I), the other is an open economy receiving positive net capital inflows (equilibrium where S + KI = I). Which statement correctly compares the two diagrams' equilibria?
choices: The open economy's effective supply-of-funds curve lies to the right of the closed economy's saving curve, producing a lower equilibrium real interest rate and a higher level of investment
The two diagrams are identical, since KI doesn't affect the loanable funds market
The open economy's effective supply curve lies to the left of the closed economy's, producing a higher equilibrium real interest rate and lower investment
The open economy has a different investment demand curve than the closed economy, while the supply curve is the same in both
correctExplanation: Positive net capital inflows add foreign funds on top of domestic saving (S + KI), shifting the effective supply-of-funds curve to the right of the closed economy's saving-only curve. This produces a lower equilibrium real interest rate and supports a higher level of investment than the closed economy could achieve with domestic saving alone -- directly reflecting Class 5's statement that capital inflows mean more investment and lower interest rates.
wrongExplanations: The S + KI = I identity shows capital inflows directly add to the funds available for investment, so the two diagrams are not identical -- KI does shift the effective supply curve.
This has the direction backwards -- ADDING capital inflows increases the funds available (shifts supply right), lowering the rate and raising investment, not the reverse.

It's the supply side (S versus S + KI) that differs between the two economies, not the investment demand curve, which reflects firms' investment decisions and isn't inherently different between an open and closed economy in this comparison.

subtopic: Saving, Interest Rates, and the Trade Balance
question: A country's national saving rate falls sharply while its investment demand is unchanged. Tracing the effects through both the loanable funds market and the NX + KI = 0 identity, what should happen to the country's real interest rate, net capital inflows, and trade balance?
choices: The real interest rate rises (saving curve shifts left), which increases net capital inflows (a movement along the capital-inflow curve), which in turn implies a trade deficit (since NX = -KI)
The real interest rate falls, net capital inflows decrease, and the country runs a trade surplus
The real interest rate rises, but net capital inflows and the trade balance are unaffected, since they're determined independently of the loanable funds market
Lower national saving directly causes a trade deficit, with no change in the real interest rate or capital inflows
correctExplanation: A fall in national saving shifts the saving curve left in the loanable funds market, raising the equilibrium real interest rate (same crowding-out logic as a larger budget deficit). That higher domestic interest rate makes domestic assets more attractive to foreign investors, increasing net capital inflows (a movement along the upward-sloping capital-inflow curve). Since NX + KI = 0, positive and rising net capital inflows imply a trade deficit -- exactly the link Class 5 draws between low national saving and trade deficits.
wrongExplanations: This has the interest rate and capital-inflow directions backwards -- lower saving raises (not lowers) the interest rate, which increases (not decreases) capital inflows, and the country ends up with a deficit, not a surplus.
Class 5 explicitly links the loanable funds market to capital flows and the trade balance through the real interest rate -- these markets are connected, not independent.
The link runs through the interest rate and capital inflows, not directly -- lower saving doesn't cause a trade deficit by itself without the interest-rate and capital-flow mechanism in between.

question: In the money market diagram shown, Md is downward-sloping and Ms is vertical. What does the vertical shape of Ms represent?
choices: The money supply is set directly by the Fed's policy choices, not by the interest rate, so it doesn't change with the interest rate
The money supply increases automatically as the interest rate rises
The money supply decreases automatically as the interest rate rises
The money supply and money demand are the same curve
correctExplanation: The Fed controls the money supply through tools like open-market bond purchases/sales and the required reserve ratio -- it doesn't passively respond to the interest rate the way money demand does. This is represented as a vertical line at whatever quantity the Fed has chosen, regardless of the interest rate (the vertical axis).
wrongExplanations: A vertical line means the quantity supplied doesn't change with the interest rate at all -- it isn't sloping upward with the rate, it's simply fixed.
Same issue -- a vertical line doesn't slope downward either; the whole point of the vertical shape is that Ms doesn't respond to the interest rate.
Md and Ms are two distinct curves that cross at the equilibrium interest rate E -- they represent different behaviors (demand for money vs. the Fed's supply choice), not the same curve.

diagram.alt: A money market diagram with quantity of money on the horizontal axis and the nominal interest rate on the vertical axis. The downward-sloping money demand curve Md and the vertical money supply line Ms cross at equilibrium point E.
diagram.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 190" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="presentation" focusable="false"><line x1="50" y1="20" x2="50" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="230" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140.0" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Quantity of Money</text><text x="12" y="90.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)" transform="rotate(-90 12 90.0)">Nominal Interest Rate</text><line x1="60" y1="30" x2="210" y2="150" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><line x1="135" y1="20" x2="135" y2="160" stroke="var(--good)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><circle cx="135" cy="90" r="3.2" fill="var(--text)"/><text x="140" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">E</text><text x="213" y="33" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Md</text><text x="140" y="25" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Ms</text></svg>

question: The Fed conducts an open-market purchase, buying government bonds from banks. In the diagram shown (Ms shifts to Ms'), what happens to the equilibrium nominal interest rate?
choices: The equilibrium nominal interest rate falls, as the money supply curve shifts right and intersects the unchanged money demand curve at a lower rate
The equilibrium nominal interest rate rises, as the money supply curve shifts left
The equilibrium nominal interest rate is unaffected, since only the Fed's balance sheet changes
The money demand curve shifts left in response, offsetting the change in supply
correctExplanation: Buying bonds injects new reserves into the banking system, increasing the money supply -- the vertical Ms line shifts right (to Ms'). With money demand unchanged, the new equilibrium E' is found where the fixed Md curve crosses the new, further-right Ms' line: a lower equilibrium nominal interest rate, exactly as Discussion Session 3 concludes for Fed bond purchases.
wrongExplanations: Buying bonds increases the money supply (shifts Ms right), not left -- this choice has both the shift direction and the resulting rate-change direction backwards.
The change in the money supply directly changes where Ms crosses Md, which changes the equilibrium interest rate -- it is not just an internal Fed accounting matter.
Money demand doesn't automatically shift in response to a money-supply change; in this simple diagram, Md stays fixed and the equilibrium moves because Ms shifted, not because both curves moved.

diagram.alt: A money market diagram with quantity of money on the horizontal axis and the nominal interest rate on the vertical axis. The money supply line shifts right from Ms to Ms', while money demand Md stays fixed. Equilibrium moves from E to E', with the interest rate falling.
diagram.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 190" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="presentation" focusable="false"><line x1="50" y1="20" x2="50" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="230" y2="160" stroke="var(--text)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140.0" y="182" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Quantity of Money</text><text x="12" y="90.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)" transform="rotate(-90 12 90.0)">Nominal Interest Rate</text><line x1="60" y1="30" x2="210" y2="150" stroke="var(--accent)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><line x1="135" y1="20" x2="135" y2="160" stroke="var(--good)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" /><line x1="165" y1="20" x2="165" y2="160" stroke="var(--good)" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/><circle cx="135" cy="90" r="3.2" fill="var(--text)"/><circle cx="165" cy="114" r="3.2" fill="var(--text)"/><text x="140" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">E</text><text x="170" y="118" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">E'</text><text x="213" y="33" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Md</text><text x="140" y="25" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Ms</text><text x="170" y="25" text-anchor="start" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--text)">Ms'</text></svg>

question: The economy enters a strong expansion: real income and the volume of transactions rise sharply, while the Fed leaves the money supply unchanged. What happens to money demand and the equilibrium nominal interest rate?
choices: Money demand shifts right (more money is needed for the higher volume of transactions), raising the equilibrium nominal interest rate
Money demand shifts left, lowering the equilibrium nominal interest rate
Money demand doesn't shift; there is only a movement along the money supply curve
The money supply curve shifts right automatically to accommodate the higher transaction volume
correctExplanation: Higher real income means more transactions, and both individuals and businesses need to hold more money to conduct them, shifting the money demand curve right at every interest rate. With the money supply held fixed by the Fed, the new equilibrium (where the shifted Md crosses the unchanged vertical Ms) occurs at a higher nominal interest rate.
wrongExplanations: A boom increases (not decreases) the need to hold money for transactions, so money demand shifts right, not left.

Real income is not on either axis of the standard money-market diagram (interest rate and quantity of money), so a change in real income shifts the money demand curve rather than causing a movement along a fixed curve.
The problem states the Fed leaves the money supply unchanged -- Ms doesn't move on its own in response to economic conditions; only deliberate Fed action shifts it.

question: A student is shown the standard money-market diagram and says: 'If the interest rate rises, money demand must shift left, since people want to hold less money at a higher rate.' What is wrong with this claim?
choices: A change in the interest rate itself is a movement along the existing money demand curve, not a shift of the curve, since the interest rate is the variable plotted on the vertical axis
Nothing is wrong -- a higher interest rate does shift money demand left
The claim is backwards -- a higher interest rate shifts money demand right
Money demand is completely unaffected by the interest rate in any way
correctExplanation: The money demand curve already slopes downward in the interest rate: at a higher interest rate, the quantity of money demanded is lower, but this is read as a movement along the existing downward-sloping curve, not a shift of the whole curve. A shift requires a change in something other than the interest rate itself -- like real income, payment technology, or risk perceptions.
wrongExplanations: This confirms the student's error rather than correcting it -- an interest-rate change alone does not shift the curve, even though it does reduce the quantity of money demanded (via a movement along the curve).
This just swaps the shift direction while keeping the same underlying mistake -- a change in the interest rate is still a movement along the curve, not a shift in either direction.

The money demand curve's whole downward slope reflects its responsiveness to the interest rate -- money demand is clearly affected by the interest rate, just via a movement along the curve rather than a shift of it.

question: The Fed raises the required reserve ratio without conducting any open-market operations. Banks' actual reserves stay the same, but the money multiplier (1/reserve ratio) falls. What happens to the money supply curve, and why?
choices: The money supply curve shifts left, because the same quantity of bank reserves now supports a smaller maximum quantity of deposits under the new, smaller multiplier
The money supply curve shifts right, because a higher reserve ratio makes banks hold more money in reserve, which counts as an increase in the money supply
The money supply curve doesn't shift, since only open-market operations (not the reserve ratio) can move Ms
The money demand curve shifts left, while the money supply curve is unaffected
correctExplanation: The money supply that a given quantity of bank reserves can support equals reserves x the money multiplier (1/reserve ratio). Raising the reserve ratio lowers the multiplier, so the same level of reserves now supports fewer total deposits system-wide -- the maximum sustainable money supply falls at every interest rate, shifting the vertical Ms line left.
wrongExplanations: Reserves sitting idle in bank vaults are explicitly NOT part of the money supply (per Class 6) -- a higher reserve requirement shrinks, not grows, the money supply banks can support through lending.
The required reserve ratio is one of the tools Discussion Session 3 identifies as affecting the money supply, alongside open-market bond purchases/sales -- it isn't limited to OMOs only.
This scenario is about a change in the money supply (via the multiplier), not money demand -- Ms is the curve that shifts here, not Md.

sourceLabel: Guest Lecture: Price Stability and the Monetary Policy of the ECB
question: In the ECB's pre-2007 interest rate corridor system, the marginal lending rate forms the ceiling and the deposit rate forms the floor around the overnight money-market rate. If the central bank supplies MORE liquidity than banks need for their minimum reserve and cash requirements, what happens to the overnight rate?
choices: It gets pushed down toward the deposit rate floor, since banks have more reserves than they need and are willing to lend them out at a lower rate
It gets pushed up toward the marginal lending rate ceiling
It stays exactly at the central bank's key policy rate regardless of how much liquidity is supplied
The corridor itself widens to absorb the extra liquidity
correctExplanation: The guest lecture explains that supplying more liquidity than needed pushes the market rate down toward the deposit facility rate (the floor of the corridor), since banks with excess reserves are willing to lend them to other banks at rates approaching the deposit rate rather than earn nothing extra by holding them idle.
wrongExplanations: Too much liquidity pushes the rate toward the FLOOR (deposit rate), not the ceiling (marginal lending rate) -- the ceiling is approached when there's too LITTLE liquidity instead.

The guest lecture explains that the rate is only pinned exactly at the central bank's key policy rate when liquidity supplied exactly matches structural needs -- oversupplying liquidity pushes it away from that central point, toward the floor.
The corridor's width (set by the marginal lending and deposit rates) doesn't change in response to liquidity operations -- it's the market rate's POSITION within the fixed corridor that moves.

sourceLabel: Guest Lecture: Price Stability and the Monetary Policy of the ECB
question: Compare the ECB's pre-2007 'corridor' system (liquidity supplied to just match structural needs, rate pinned near the center of the corridor) to its post-2008 'floor' system (persistent excess liquidity in the banking system). How does the overnight rate's position differ between the two?
choices: In the corridor system, the rate sits near the center (close to the key policy rate); in the floor system, persistent excess liquidity pushes the rate down to sit at the deposit rate (the floor) instead
In both systems, the rate sits at the same position -- near the center of the corridor
In the corridor system, the rate sits at the floor; in the floor system, it sits at the ceiling
The floor system eliminates the corridor entirely, so there is no ceiling or floor rate anymore
correctExplanation: The guest lecture describes the pre-2007 system as balancing liquidity supply to pin the rate near the center of the corridor (close to the main policy rate), while the post-2008 system operates with persistent excess liquidity, which -- following the same 'too much liquidity pushes the rate toward the floor' logic -- has driven the overnight rate down to sit at the deposit facility rate, making that deposit rate the de facto key policy rate today.
wrongExplanations: The two systems are explicitly contrasted in the lecture -- the corridor system centers the rate, while the floor system's excess liquidity pushes it down to the floor; they are not the same.
This reverses the two systems -- it's the FLOOR system (not the corridor system) where the rate sits at the deposit floor; the corridor system pins it near the center, not at the floor.

The corridor (marginal lending rate and deposit rate) still formally exists as the boundary in the floor system -- what changed is where within that corridor the rate actually sits, not that the corridor was removed.

question: Class 4 describes diminishing returns to capital: as more capital is added (holding other inputs fixed), output increases, but by less and less with each additional unit of capital. Which shape best describes the resulting curve of total output plotted against the quantity of capital?
choices: An upward-sloping curve that becomes flatter as capital increases -- output keeps rising but at a decreasing rate
An upward-sloping straight line -- output rises by the same amount for each additional unit of capital
A downward-sloping curve -- output falls as capital increases
An upward-sloping curve that becomes steeper as capital increases -- output rises by more with each additional unit
correctExplanation: Diminishing returns means each additional unit of capital adds LESS to output than the previous unit did, while total output still increases overall. Graphically, this is a curve that keeps rising (slopes upward) but flattens out as capital increases -- a concave, bowed-down shape, not a straight line or a curve that gets steeper.
wrongExplanations: A straight line would mean each additional unit of capital adds the SAME amount to output -- constant, not diminishing, returns.
Output still increases with more capital under diminishing returns; it's the SIZE of each increase that shrinks, not the overall direction of the curve.
A curve that gets steeper would represent INCREASING returns to capital (each unit adding more than the last), the opposite of diminishing returns.

question: Two countries start with the same GDP per capita and grow at different constant annual rates for 200 years, as in Class 4's compound-growth illustration. Plotting each country's GDP per capita against time, how does the gap between the two curves behave?
choices: The gap grows increasingly larger over time -- the curves diverge at an accelerating rate, not a constant one
The gap grows by the same fixed amount every year -- the curves diverge at a constant rate
The gap stays the same size throughout the 200 years
The gap shrinks over time as the faster-growing country's growth rate eventually slows on its own
correctExplanation: Class 4's own example (comparing $10 growing at 2%, 4%, and 6% over 210 years to wildly different final values) illustrates that compound growth is exponential, not linear -- a small, constant difference in growth RATES compounds into an ever-widening ABSOLUTE gap over time, so the two countries' GDP-per-capita curves diverge at an accelerating (not constant) pace.
wrongExplanations: Constant-rate divergence would describe simple (linear) growth, not compound growth -- compound growth's whole point is that a fixed percentage difference produces an accelerating, not constant, absolute gap over time.
A constant-percentage growth-rate difference compounds over time rather than canceling out, so the gap does not stay the same size -- it widens.
Nothing in Class 4's compound growth formula causes a country's growth rate to slow down on its own over time; the rates are assumed constant, so the gap keeps widening rather than shrinking.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A small closed economy's households and firms save a combined $2.4 billion this year. The government's budget is not balanced -- it spends $350 million more than it collects in net taxes. Using the fact that in a closed economy investment equals national saving, what is this economy's investment level this year?
choices: $2.05 billion
$2.75 billion
$2.4 billion
$350 million
correctExplanation: In a closed economy, national saving equals investment. The government spending more than it collects is a budget deficit -- public dissaving -- which reduces national saving below private saving alone: $2.4 billion - $350 million = $2.05 billion, so investment is $2.05 billion.
wrongExplanations: This adds the deficit to private saving instead of subtracting it -- a budget deficit is public dissaving, which reduces national saving, not increases it.
This uses only the combined private saving figure and ignores the government's budget deficit entirely.
This is just the size of the deficit, not the economy's total national saving/investment.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: To finance a new food truck, an entrepreneur borrows $15,000 from a bank at a fixed 8% annual interest rate. The full loan, plus all accumulated interest, is due as a single payment 5 years later. Over those 5 years, prices rise by an average of 3% per year. In today's (inflation-adjusted) dollars, how large is that final payment expected to be?
correctExplanation: The real interest rate is the nominal rate minus inflation: 8% - 3% = 5%. In today's (real) dollars, the loan compounds at this real rate over the 5 years: $15,000 x (1.05)^5, which is approximately $19,144.
wrongExplanations: This compounds the loan at the NOMINAL rate (8%) instead of the real rate -- since the question asks for the payment in today's dollars, inflation must be stripped out first using the real rate.
This uses the correct 5% real rate but applies it as simple (non-compounding) interest instead of compounding it over the 5 years.
This is just the original loan amount, with no interest applied at all.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Over a multi-year period, the value of many households' stock portfolios rises substantially, even though their income and day-to-day spending habits are otherwise unchanged. What is the most likely effect of these capital gains on the (measured) household saving rate?
choices: The saving rate falls, since households feel wealthier and treat the capital gains as a substitute for actively saving out of income
The saving rate rises, since capital gains are themselves counted as additional saving out of income
The saving rate is unaffected, since saving is defined only in terms of income and current consumption, which haven't changed
The saving rate rises because households become more cautious after a large wealth increase and cut back on consumption
correctExplanation: Rising asset prices are a form of capital gains -- an increase in the value of existing assets, not new saving out of current income. Households often treat these gains as a substitute for actively saving out of their paycheck, so the measured saving rate (income minus consumption, divided by income) tends to fall even as wealth rises.
wrongExplanations: Capital gains are a change in asset value, not income saved out of a paycheck -- the standard measure of saving (income minus consumption spending) does not directly include unrealized capital gains.

While the definition of saving doesn't change, the behavioral response does: feeling wealthier from capital gains tends to make households consume more (and therefore save less) out of a given income, not leave saving unaffected.

This describes the opposite behavioral response (a precautionary reaction) from what actually tends to happen -- rising wealth typically makes households feel more, not less, comfortable spending, which lowers rather than raises saving.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A logging company sells $25,000 worth of raw lumber to a furniture manufacturer. The manufacturer turns the lumber into finished tables and chairs and sells them to a wholesale distributor for $90,000. The distributor sells the furniture to retail stores for $130,000, and the retail stores sell it to the public for $180,000. Using the value-added method, what is the total contribution to GDP from this chain?
correctExplanation: Value added at each stage: the logging company contributes $25,000 (no purchased inputs), the manufacturer contributes $90,000 - $25,000 = $65,000, the distributor contributes $130,000 - $90,000 = $40,000, and the retail stores contribute $180,000 - $130,000 = $50,000. Total value added = $25,000 + $65,000 + $40,000 + $50,000 = $180,000 -- equal to the final sale price to the public, as it must be.
wrongExplanations: This sums all four firms' full revenues ($25,000 + $90,000 + $130,000 + $180,000) instead of each firm's value added, double- and triple-counting the lumber and furniture along the way.
This is only the distributor's sale price to retail stores, not the total value added across the whole chain.
This is only the retail stores' own value added, ignoring the value added by the other three firms earlier in the chain.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: In a multi-firm production chain, why does summing the value added at each stage give the same GDP total as simply taking the market value of the final good sold to the public?
choices: Because each firm's value added excludes the cost of intermediate goods it purchased from earlier firms, so summing value added avoids counting the same output more than once, leaving only the final good's total value
Because every firm in the chain always adds exactly the same dollar amount of value, so the sum trivially equals the final price
Because value added only counts labor costs, and the final sale price is also just the sum of labor costs across firms
Because intermediate goods are excluded from GDP entirely, so only the value-added method captures any of the transactions in the chain
correctExplanation: Value added at each stage equals that firm's revenue minus what it paid for inputs from earlier firms, so intermediate purchases are subtracted out exactly once at each step. Summing these value-added figures telescopes down to just the final sale price -- the same total you'd get by counting only the final good sold to the public, without double-counting the intermediate transactions along the way.
wrongExplanations: Firms typically add very different amounts of value at each stage; the two methods still agree even when value added differs firm to firm, because of how intermediate costs are subtracted, not because every firm adds an identical amount.

Value added includes all costs a firm doesn't pay to other firms in the chain (labor, capital income, profits), not labor costs alone -- and the final sale price is a market price, not a sum of labor costs.

Intermediate goods aren't excluded from every stage of the chain -- they just aren't counted twice; the value-added method still incorporates their value indirectly, at the stage where they are transformed and resold.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A retiree's monthly pension was set at $1,200 in a year when the CPI stood at 110. The CPI has since climbed to 176. To restore the pension to its original real (inflation-adjusted) purchasing power, what monthly amount should it be updated to?
correctExplanation: Indexing scales a nominal amount by the ratio of the new price index to the old one: $1,200 x (176/110) = $1,200 x 1.6 = $1,920.
wrongExplanations: This leaves the pension unchanged, ignoring the rise in prices entirely -- with no adjustment, its real purchasing power would have fallen.
This inverts the CPI ratio (110/176 instead of 176/110), which understates the pension needed to preserve purchasing power.
This multiplies by 1.76 (treating the new CPI as if it were already a ratio) instead of by the correct ratio of 176 to the base value of 110.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Suppose average labor productivity in a country grew at a constant 3.0% per year from 1965 to 1995 (30 years). If that same 3.0% growth rate had instead continued for a longer 40-year period (1965 to 2005), productivity in 2005 would have been approximately what multiple of its 1965 level?
choices: about 3.26 times
about 2.43 times
about 2.20 times
about 1.03 times
correctExplanation: Compound growth over 40 years at 3.0% per year: (1.03)^40 is approximately 3.26 -- productivity would be about 3.26 times its 1965 level.
wrongExplanations: This is (1.03)^30, the multiple for the original 30-year period, not the extended 40-year period the question asks about.
This uses simple (non-compounding) growth -- 1 + (0.03 x 40) -- instead of compounding the 3.0% rate over 40 years.
This is just 1 plus the annual rate (1.03), as if applying it for only a single year instead of compounding over 40 years.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A country's average labor productivity grew at 2.5% per year from 1965-1980 (15 years), then slowed to 1.0% per year from 1980-2010 (30 years). If productivity had instead continued growing at the initial 2.5% rate for the full 45-year period (1965-2010), approximately how much higher would 2010 productivity have been under that counterfactual, compared to the actual 2010 level (after the slowdown)?
choices: about 55.6% higher
about 7.5% higher
about 11.8% higher
about 26.9% higher
correctExplanation: Counterfactual (2.5% for the full 45 years): (1.025)^45 is about 3.038 times the 1965 level. Actual (2.5% for 15 years, then 1.0% for 30 years): (1.025)^15 x (1.010)^30 is about 1.952 times the 1965 level. The counterfactual is 3.038 / 1.952 is about 1.556 times the actual level -- about 55.6% higher.
wrongExplanations: This compounds the counterfactual for only 30 years (matching the length of the second, slower period) instead of the full 45-year span the question asks about.
This incorrectly averages the two growth rates into one flat 1.75% rate applied for 45 years, instead of compounding each sub-period separately at its own rate.
This uses simple (non-compounding) interest for both the counterfactual and the actual figures instead of compound growth.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: An economy that engages in no trade with the rest of the world produces $7,500 billion in total output this year. Households and businesses spend $5,200 billion of after-tax income on consumption. The government collects $1,350 billion more in taxes than it pays out in transfer payments, and spends $1,100 billion on government purchases. What are this economy's private saving, public saving, and national saving, respectively?
correctExplanation: Private saving = total output - net taxes - consumption = 7,500 - 1,350 - 5,200 = 950. Public saving = net taxes - government purchases = 1,350 - 1,100 = 250. National saving = private + public = 950 + 250 = 1,200 (matches total output - consumption - government purchases = 7,500 - 5,200 - 1,100 = 1,200).
wrongExplanations: This correctly computes private and public saving but doesn't add them together for national saving.
This miscalculates private saving, which overstates national saving as a result.
This flips the sign on public saving -- with taxes exceeding government purchases here, the government runs a surplus (positive public saving), not a deficit.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Continuing the same economy from the previous question (total output $7,500 billion, private saving fixed at $950 billion regardless of the interest rate, and net taxes unchanged at $1,350 billion): the government raises its purchases from $1,100 billion to $1,400 billion. What is the new level of national saving?
choices: $900 billion
$1,200 billion
$1,000 billion
-$50 billion
correctExplanation: Public saving is now net taxes - government purchases = $1,350 billion - $1,400 billion = -$50 billion. With private saving unchanged at $950 billion, national saving = $950 billion + (-$50 billion) = $900 billion.
wrongExplanations: This is the original national saving figure -- it doesn't account for the increase in government purchases, which lowers public saving.
This flips the sign on the new public saving figure (using government purchases minus net taxes = $50 billion instead of -$50 billion), overstating national saving.
This reports only the new (negative) public saving figure by itself, not the full national saving total (private plus public).

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Continuing the same economy: assume the aggregate saving-supply curve in the loanable funds market is a vertical line (national saving does not respond to the real interest rate), while investment demand is downward-sloping as usual. When the government's increased purchases reduce national saving from $1,200 to $900 (as in the previous question), the vertical saving-supply line shifts left, as shown in the diagram (S to S'). What happens to the equilibrium real interest rate and the level of investment?
choices: The real interest rate rises, and investment falls to $900 (matching the new lower national saving, since investment must still equal national saving in a closed economy)
The real interest rate falls, and investment rises
Neither the real interest rate nor investment changes, since a vertical saving-supply curve is completely insensitive to the interest rate
The real interest rate rises, but investment stays at $1,200 since the investment demand curve itself didn't shift
correctExplanation: Even though the saving-supply curve is vertical (doesn't respond to r), its position still shifts left when national saving falls, from $1,200 to $900. Since it's a closed economy, investment must equal national saving in equilibrium, so investment falls to $900 too. Reading this new, lower quantity off the downward-sloping investment demand curve requires a higher real interest rate -- the same qualitative result as an upward-sloping saving curve shifting left, just via a vertical curve's position moving instead of a slope-preserving parallel shift.
wrongExplanations: This has both directions backwards -- a leftward shift in the saving-supply line (a decrease in the funds available) raises the equilibrium interest rate and lowers investment, not the reverse.

The curve's insensitivity to r (its vertical shape) is different from the curve's position being fixed -- the vertical line can still shift left or right when the total quantity of saving supplied changes, even though its slope never changes.

Investment demand doesn't shift, but the equilibrium level of investment still changes, because in a closed economy investment must equal whatever the new equilibrium level of saving is; a movement to a new point along the fixed investment demand curve is exactly how that adjustment happens.

diagram.alt: A loanable funds diagram with saving and investment on the horizontal axis and the real interest rate on the vertical axis. A vertical saving-supply line shifts left from S to S', while the downward-sloping investment demand curve I stays fixed. Equilibrium moves from E to E', with the real interest rate rising and the quantity of saving and investment falling.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A furniture retailer built up $2.4 million worth of unsold inventory during 2019 (counted as inventory investment in 2019's GDP). In 2020, the retailer sells off this entire stockpile to customers, with no new furniture produced that year. How does this $2.4 million in sales affect 2020's GDP?
choices: It has no effect on 2020 GDP -- the furniture was already counted as production (inventory investment) in 2019, and selling it in 2020 is offset by a matching drawdown in inventory investment
It adds the full $2.4 million to 2020 GDP as consumption spending
It subtracts $2.4 million from 2020 GDP, since inventory investment reverses
It adds $2.4 million to 2020 GDP, but only if the furniture was sold at its original production cost
correctExplanation: GDP counts goods in the year they are produced, not the year they are sold. The $2.4 million was already counted as inventory investment (a component of I) when the furniture was produced/stockpiled in 2019. When it's sold in 2020, consumption spending (C) rises by $2.4 million, but inventory investment (I) falls by the same $2.4 million as the stockpile is drawn down -- the two effects exactly offset, leaving 2020 GDP unaffected by this sale.
wrongExplanations: This ignores the offsetting drop in inventory investment; consumption rising by $2.4 million is exactly canceled by inventory investment falling by $2.4 million in the same year.
GDP doesn't fall either -- the rise in consumption spending and the fall in inventory investment net out to zero, not a net negative.
The sale price doesn't matter to this GDP-timing question; regardless of price, the furniture's contribution to GDP was already recorded in 2019 when it was produced, not when it's eventually sold.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A collector purchases a rare, non-dividend-paying share of a private company for $500, expecting to hold it for three years before selling. Comparable safe investments currently yield 3% per year, and investors demand an extra 7 percentage points of return to compensate for this particular stock's risk. Assuming the $500 purchase price reflects the present value of the expected future sale price, what sale price would justify paying $500 today?
correctExplanation: The discount rate for a risky asset is the safe rate plus the risk premium: 3% + 7% = 10%. Solving the present-value equation for the future price over 3 years: $500 = X / (1.10)^3, so X = $500 x (1.10)^3, approximately $665.50.
wrongExplanations: This uses only the safe rate (3%) as the discount rate, ignoring the extra return investors demand for the stock's risk.
This uses only the risk premium (7%) as the discount rate, ignoring the safe rate that should also be included.
This applies the correct 10% discount rate but as simple (non-compounding) interest over the three years instead of compounding it.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A country's real GDP was $4,200 billion in 1995 and grew to $6,800 billion by 2015 (a 20-year period). What was the approximate compound annual growth rate of real GDP over this period?
choices: about 2.4%
about 3.1%
about 61.9%
about 1.2%
correctExplanation: The compound annual growth rate solves ($6,800 / $4,200) = (1+g)^20 for g: g = (6,800/4,200)^(1/20) - 1, which is approximately 2.4%.
wrongExplanations: This mistakenly divides the total percentage change over the period (about 61.9%) by the number of years, treating growth as simple/linear rather than compound.
This is the total percentage change in real GDP over the full 20 years, without annualizing it into a yearly compound growth rate.
This mistakenly uses 40 years in the exponent instead of the actual 20-year period, roughly halving the correctly implied annual rate.

subtopic: Foreign Shocks and U.S. Capital Inflows
sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: The fictional country of Nortavia experiences a period of rapid income growth. As Nortavian households and firms consume and invest more of their own rising income at home, less of Nortavia's saving is available to flow abroad as capital sent to the United States. Holding everything else in the U.S. loanable funds market constant, what happens to the equilibrium U.S. real interest rate and the level of U.S. investment?
choices: U.S. net capital inflows fall, shifting the U.S. effective supply-of-funds curve (S + KI) left; the U.S. real interest rate rises and U.S. investment falls
U.S. net capital inflows rise, shifting the effective supply curve right; the U.S. real interest rate falls and U.S. investment rises
U.S. investment demand shifts left, while the supply of funds is unaffected
There is no effect on the U.S. loanable funds market, since Nortavia's saving decisions are a purely domestic Nortavian matter
correctExplanation: Less Nortavian saving flowing abroad means fewer capital inflows into the U.S. (lower KI), which reduces the effective supply of funds available for U.S. investment (S + KI shifts left, since KI is now a smaller positive addition to domestic saving S). A leftward shift in the effective supply of loanable funds raises the equilibrium U.S. real interest rate and lowers the equilibrium level of U.S. investment -- the same crowding-out-style logic as a domestic saving decrease, just driven by a foreign shock instead.
wrongExplanations: This has the direction backwards -- Nortavia sending less capital abroad means U.S. capital inflows fall, not rise, which shifts the effective U.S. supply curve left, not right.
The shock changes how much foreign saving is available to fund U.S. investment (a supply-side effect via KI), not U.S. firms' own demand for investment funds -- investment demand is unaffected here.

Capital inflows are exactly the channel connecting a foreign country's saving decisions to the U.S. loanable funds market -- via S + KI = I, a change in foreign capital available to the U.S. does have a direct effect on the U.S. equilibrium interest rate and investment.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: For most of the past several decades, the United States has run a trade deficit (negative net exports) while financing it through positive net capital inflows. Which statement correctly describes this pattern using the identity NX + KI = 0?
choices: NX is negative and KI is positive: the U.S. imports more than it exports, and finances the difference by selling domestic assets to foreigners
NX is positive and KI is negative: the U.S. exports more than it imports, and lends the surplus abroad
Both NX and KI are negative, since a trade deficit means the U.S. is losing funds on both sides of the identity
NX and KI are both positive, since the identity requires them to move in the same direction
correctExplanation: The U.S. has run persistent trade deficits, meaning NX (exports minus imports) is negative. Since NX + KI = 0, a negative NX requires a positive KI -- positive net capital inflows, meaning foreigners are net purchasers of U.S. assets (stocks, bonds, real estate), which is exactly how the U.S. finances buying more from the rest of the world than it sells.
wrongExplanations: This describes the pattern of a country running a trade surplus and lending abroad (like a net exporter), which is the opposite of the U.S.'s actual longstanding trade-deficit pattern.
The identity NX + KI = 0 means the two must sum to exactly zero, so they cannot both be negative (or both positive) at the same time -- one must be positive when the other is negative.
The identity requires NX and KI to have opposite signs (so they sum to zero), not the same sign.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Among loans, bonds, stocks, and money, which is distinguished by paying a nominal interest rate of exactly zero?
choices: Money
Bonds
Stocks
Loans
correctExplanation: Money (cash and similar non-interest-bearing balances) is defined as an asset with a zero nominal interest rate -- it is held for its liquidity as a medium of exchange, not for a direct interest return, unlike bonds (which pay coupon interest), loans (which charge interest), or stocks (which can pay dividends and offer capital gains).
wrongExplanations: Bonds pay coupon interest as compensation for lending funds to the issuer -- they are not a zero-interest asset.
Stocks can pay dividends and offer capital gains as a return; they are not defined by a zero nominal interest rate.
Loans charge the borrower interest as the lender's compensation -- they are not a zero-interest asset either.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A closed economy's investment this year totals $3.6 billion. The government's budget shows a $420 million deficit. Using the closed-economy identity that investment equals national saving, what is the combined saving of this economy's households and firms (private saving)?
choices: $4.02 billion
$3.18 billion
$3.6 billion
$420 million
correctExplanation: In a closed economy, investment equals national saving, and national saving is private saving plus public saving. A budget deficit is negative public saving, so private saving = investment + deficit = $3.6 billion + $420 million = $4.02 billion.
wrongExplanations: This subtracts the deficit from investment instead of adding it back -- since the deficit already pulled national saving (and therefore investment) down, private saving must be larger than investment, not smaller.
This reports investment itself, ignoring that a budget deficit means private saving must exceed the investment/national-saving total.
This is just the size of the deficit, not the economy's private saving.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: In a closed economy, households and firms save a combined $1.8 billion this year. The government runs a budget surplus of $300 million (it collects more in net taxes than it spends). What is this economy's investment level?
choices: $2.1 billion
$1.5 billion
$1.8 billion
$300 million
correctExplanation: A budget surplus is positive public saving, which adds to private saving to get national saving (and, in a closed economy, investment): $1.8 billion + $300 million = $2.1 billion.
wrongExplanations: This subtracts the surplus instead of adding it -- a surplus is positive public saving and should increase national saving, not decrease it.
This uses only private saving and ignores the government surplus entirely.
This is just the size of the surplus, not the economy's total investment.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: In a closed economy, households and firms save $2.0 billion, and the government runs a budget deficit of $600 million. A student argues that investment must be $2.6 billion, reasoning that "more government borrowing means more total funds available for investment." Evaluate this reasoning.
choices: The reasoning is wrong: a budget deficit is public dissaving, which subtracts from private saving to get national saving/investment, so investment is $1.4 billion, not $2.6 billion
The reasoning is correct: government borrowing adds directly to the pool of loanable funds, so investment is $2.6 billion
The reasoning is wrong, but investment is still $2.0 billion, since the government's budget position doesn't affect national saving
The reasoning is wrong, but investment equals $600 million, the size of the deficit itself
correctExplanation: A budget deficit means the government is borrowing, not supplying funds -- it is public dissaving. National saving (and, in a closed economy, investment) is private saving plus public saving: $2.0 billion + (-$600 million) = $1.4 billion. The deficit reduces the funds available for investment rather than adding to them.
wrongExplanations: This restates the student's flawed reasoning -- government borrowing competes for loanable funds rather than adding to their supply.
This correctly rejects the student's number but incorrectly ignores the deficit's effect on national saving; public saving is part of the national-saving identity and cannot be dropped.
This misidentifies the deficit itself as the level of investment, rather than as one component that reduces national saving from its private-saving starting point.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A small business borrows $22,000 at a fixed 9% annual interest rate. The full loan, plus all accumulated interest, is due as a single payment 6 years later. Over those 6 years, prices rise by an average of 4% per year. In today's (inflation-adjusted) dollars, how large is that final payment expected to be?
correctExplanation: The real interest rate is the nominal rate minus inflation: 9% - 4% = 5%. In today's (real) dollars, the loan compounds at this real rate over 6 years: $22,000 x (1.05)^6, which is approximately $29,482.
wrongExplanations: This compounds the loan at the NOMINAL rate (9%) instead of the real rate -- since the question asks for the payment in today's dollars, inflation must be stripped out first using the real rate.
This uses the correct 5% real rate but applies it as simple (non-compounding) interest instead of compounding it over the 6 years.
This is just the original loan amount, with no interest applied at all.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: An investor deposits $8,000 in an account with a fixed 7% nominal annual interest rate. After 4 years, the account balance is worth approximately $9,004 in today's purchasing power. Approximately what was the average annual inflation rate over these 4 years?
choices: about 4%
about 7%
about 10%
about 3%
correctExplanation: First find the real rate that turns $8,000 into $9,004 over 4 years: (1+r)^4 = 9,004/8,000 = 1.1255, so r is approximately 3%. Since the real rate equals the nominal rate minus inflation, inflation = nominal rate - real rate = 7% - 3% = 4%.
wrongExplanations: This is just the nominal interest rate itself, without subtracting out the real growth the deposit actually achieved.
This adds the nominal rate and the implied real rate (7% + 3%) instead of subtracting the real rate from the nominal rate.
This reports the implied real rate (about 3%) as if it were the inflation rate, rather than using it to back out inflation from the nominal rate.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Two savers each deposit $10,000 for 3 years. Saver A earns a fixed 6% nominal annual rate while inflation averages 2% per year. Saver B earns a fixed 8% nominal annual rate while inflation averages 5% per year. In real (inflation-adjusted) terms, which saver ends up with more purchasing power, and by roughly how much?
choices: Saver A, by about $322, because A's real interest rate (4%) is higher than B's (3%), despite B's higher nominal rate
Saver B, by about $322, because B's nominal rate (8%) is higher than A's (6%)
Saver B, because a higher inflation rate directly boosts real investment returns
They end up with identical real purchasing power, since both started with the same $10,000 deposit
correctExplanation: Real rates are nominal minus inflation: Saver A earns 6% - 2% = 4% real, Saver B earns 8% - 5% = 3% real. Over 3 years, A's real value is $10,000 x (1.04)^3 is about $11,249, while B's is $10,000 x (1.03)^3 is about $10,927 -- A ends up ahead by about $322, because A's real rate is higher even though B's nominal rate is higher.
wrongExplanations: This picks the right winner's approximate margin but the wrong saver -- comparing nominal rates directly is misleading once the two savers face different inflation rates.
Higher inflation erodes real returns; it does not boost them. B's higher inflation rate is exactly why B's real rate ends up lower than A's despite B's higher nominal rate.
The two real rates (4% vs. 3%) are different, so the two savers do not end up with the same real purchasing power after 3 years of compounding.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A borrower says: "My loan charges 8% interest and inflation is 3%, so in real terms I'm paying back 11% more each year." What is wrong with this reasoning?
choices: The real rate is approximately nominal minus inflation (about 5% here), not nominal plus inflation; the borrower added instead of subtracted inflation
The real rate is approximately nominal plus inflation, so 11% is the correct real rate
Inflation doesn't affect what the borrower actually owes, so the real rate is just the nominal 8%
The real rate equals inflation minus the nominal rate, so it would be -5%, meaning the loan effectively pays the borrower
correctExplanation: The (approximate) real interest rate is the nominal rate minus the inflation rate: 8% - 3% = 5%. The borrower incorrectly added the inflation rate to the nominal rate instead of subtracting it, overstating the true real cost of the loan.
wrongExplanations: This repeats the borrower's exact mistake -- adding inflation to the nominal rate -- instead of correctly subtracting it.
Inflation directly affects the real burden of a fixed nominal loan: it erodes the real value of what's owed, so the real rate paid is lower than the nominal rate, not equal to it.

This flips the correct subtraction order; the real rate is nominal minus inflation, not inflation minus nominal, and a real loan cost is not typically negative when the nominal rate exceeds inflation.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Household A sets aside an extra $5,000 of this year's paycheck that it doesn't spend. Household B doesn't change its income or spending at all this year, but the value of the house it already owns rises by $30,000 due to a hot local housing market. How does each event affect this year's measured household saving (income minus consumption)?
choices: Household A's $5,000 counts as saving; Household B's $30,000 does not, since saving is measured from current income and consumption, not from changes in the value of assets already owned
Both amounts count equally as saving this year, since both increase household wealth by a similar order of magnitude
Neither amount counts as saving, since saving requires actually depositing cash in a formal bank account
Household B's $30,000 counts as saving, but Household A's $5,000 does not, since capital gains are a larger and more durable form of wealth building
correctExplanation: Saving is defined as current income minus current consumption. Household A's unspent $5,000 fits this definition directly. Household B's $30,000 is a capital gain -- a rise in the value of an asset it already owned -- which is not income earned or saved this year, even though it does raise the household's overall wealth.
wrongExplanations: Wealth and saving are related but distinct: a capital gain raises wealth without being counted as saving out of current income, so the two amounts do not count equally as saving.
Saving simply requires income exceeding consumption; it doesn't require the unspent amount to sit in any particular type of account.
This reverses which amount fits the definition of saving -- capital gains are a wealth effect, not saving, regardless of their size relative to income saved.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: A classmate claims: "Home values have risen for decades, so this must mean U.S. households have been actively saving more and more of their income." Evaluate this claim.
choices: The claim is false: rising home values are capital gains, not income-based saving, and in practice rising housing wealth has been associated with households saving LESS of their income, not more
The claim is true: rising asset values are the primary way the measured household saving rate increases over time
The claim is true, but only because rising home values eventually get spent on home improvements, which counts as investment spending
The claim cannot be evaluated, since the household saving rate is unrelated to housing markets in any way
correctExplanation: Rising home values are capital gains -- increases in the value of an asset already owned -- not saving out of current income. Empirically, when households feel wealthier from rising home values, they tend to treat those gains as a substitute for actively saving, which is associated with a LOWER measured saving rate, the opposite of the classmate's claim.
wrongExplanations: This gets the relationship backwards; capital gains are not counted as saving, and the associated wealth effect tends to reduce, not raise, the saving rate.
Home improvement spending is a separate decision from the capital gain itself and isn't the mechanism connecting rising home values to household saving behavior.
The housing market's effect on household wealth and spending decisions is directly relevant to (and tends to lower) the measured household saving rate.

sourceLabel: Instructor Midterm Study Guide and Practice Problems
question: Which term specifically describes an increase in the market value of an asset a household already owns (such as a home or a share of stock), as distinct from unconsumed current income?
choices: Capital gain
Private saving
Disposable income
Public saving
correctExplanation: A capital gain is an increase in the value of an existing asset. It is a change in wealth, not income earned or saved out of a paycheck, which is why it is treated differently from saving in national accounting.
